text: Gratitude to God is considered a distinctive feature of the Christian faith for those who experience the graciousness of God. The theological picture of thanksgiving as revealed in 1 Corinthians 1:4-9 and Paul’s commands concerning the behavior of the early Christians in Corinth are the focuses of this research. The apostle Paul had grounds to give thanks to God for the Corinthians because they were called to life by God from a state of death, an accomplishment which God alone made possible through His gracious calling. This study applies the descriptive qualitative research methodology to the scripture and demonstrates that it is still relevant in our time in that it is a very useful tool for assessing and promoting the believer’s spiritual growth – whether it be ancient or contemporary. The study reveals that the Corinthian believers were comfortable with the thought of their divine calling because they felt confident in the gracious call of God which had provided them with adequate sufficient grace in Jesus Christ through whom they were called and thus could give thanks to God. In the study, the apostle Paul’s exposition on thanksgiving for God’s grace is revealed to be most relevant and that Paul’s prescriptions for thanksgiving to God can still be very relevant for believers to realize God’s grace in their everyday lives.
